data.title: KCE: Not So Convinced By The AI Scare
data.description:
We cover quite a lot of capital markets players that feature in the State Street® SPDR® S&P® Capital Markets ETF (KCE), so the first thing we can comment on is the somewhat meaningful effect that these latest AI-scare trades have been having on the broader capital markets indices. The beginning of the year cemented some of the positive trends that usually emerge from lower costs of capital, namely more IPO activity usually correlated to higher market valuations and also a general return in corporate action that generates fees for financial services picks. Higher valuations have helped asset management companies too.

The KCE contains three broad sub-industries:

  • Asset management and custody banks (almost 50%)
  • Investment banking and brokerage (~30%) which is sensitive to M&A and capital markets transaction volumes and sizes, but also to countercyclical volatility in the public markets businesses
  • Financial exchanges and data providers (~20%)

that are all differently affected by the emergence of some recent LLM based AI and the overall AI-scare trade that's reared its head in markets last week. Our comment is that commodification had already come for a lot of these industries, particularly AM and exchanges, with basic automation, and we aren't sure that LLMs or AI would necessarily displace that much more revenue or steepen declines more than automation and index funds and the like already have. In other areas, we think the models are fundamentally very resilient to LLM-based AI. We aren't taking the AI threat, particularly as it has materialised in headlines now - these concerns could have been just as valid a year ago - very seriously at all. Perhaps that will prove foolish but it is our feeling on the industry.

We are therefore not negative on KCE after the sell off, though we note it's procyclical and therefore have our reservations due to our concerns about how broader markets might perform on AI-bubble fears. In particular, we are worried about growth concerns on pressure on the job market which can negatively transmit into consumer confidence and the overall economy. Therefore, we prefer countercyclical bets, which KCE is ultimately not.

KCE Breakdown

The main thing really was the aforementioned sub-industry breakdown. This is our position on the AI-scare trade with respect to each sub-industry.

  • For asset management our feeling is that equity markets had been improving and that was an important AUM and therefore profit driver. Perhaps the AI scare might affect broader market values, and this would be a threat. But in terms of the technological risk, much of asset management has already been significantly commodified. Active management has lost its lustre a long time ago, and most are quasi-indexing anyway. We don't think AI is key is accelerating this at all. If anything, LLMs may make it easier for AM firms to generate and market their quasi-indexing solutions, which we don't believe require serious insight anyway. LLMs may already provide sufficient quality for the vast majority of so-called active strategies. LLMs may also make an actually differentiated strategy more rare and could be good for the AM companies actually doing a proper job.
  • For investment banking and brokerage, increased mix of retail activity spurred by a democratisation of technology that gives confidence (perhaps not insight though) to the general public could be good for public markets brokerage. For investment banking, we think that on the supply side, banks need to keep training analysts in order to commodify senior bankers which generate most of the cost down the line after a lot of industry attrition up the ranks. On the demand side, we don't see clients replacing bankers who ultimately do provide a needed layer of due diligence and attention to detail, as well as a lot of non-public know-how and network value.
  • Financial data and exchange companies should have more value in a market with more participation from LLMs. Data is obviously valuable for training all sorts of models, and exchanges benefit much in the same way public market brokers would.

Bottom Line

Our biggest gripe with KCE is that there are very different companies within capital markets. Public brokerage is countercyclical, while IB outside of restructuring is pro-cyclical. Some businesses are human capital intensive like IB and others like exchanges are very automated in most cases. The current environment is more uncertain than usual, it would make more sense with that conviction to cut out stocks that are pro-cyclical. Specifically, the pro-cyclical sensitivity in this ETF would be the asset management businesses like for example Wisdomtree (WT) or investment banks like Perella Weinberg (PWP). Picks to keep would be brokerage stocks, like Interactive Brokers (IBKR) in terms of earnings direction, and exchange like the CME Group (CME), which are countercyclical bets in that they are pro-volatility bets typical of downmarkets. An ETF that captures this more but not the pro-cyclical elements of the KCE is the iShares U.S. Broker-Dealers & Securities Exchanges ETF (IAI) at PE 21x, which is higher than the KCE's 14.4x for the reason that it is more recession resistant and a good hedge in a downmarket.

On the other hand there are parts of capital markets that are seeing latent growth like aspects of advisory, such as ECM, which has been doing quite well, though is low visibility and ultimately pro-cyclical. ECM deals get suddenly shelved when markets go sour.

We just aren't that keen on the concept of capital markets as a single factor which is what the KCE captures, since we don't think it's specific enough or takes sufficiently into account very diverse economics of the constituent businesses. We also do note the strong correlation with the (IYF) which is a broad financial ETF and therefore must say that KCE is ultimately more procyclical than not. More fundamentally, more than 50% of the allocation is to AM and advisory indexed to IB and M&A which are definitely procyclical businesses. That being the case, with the AI scare trade still affecting markets and reflecting a serious concern about displaced labour and services, we think that there is downside to the consumer economy in the US as job cuts surge a bit and hiring remains very mute. We would not make a pro-cycle bet in 2026 which is what KCE ultimately is, and we'd prefer to focus on the subset of the KCE that does well in a volatile market.

data.title: Palmer Square Capital BDC: Trading At A Massive Discount For A Reason
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered Palmer Square Capital BDC (PSBD), I issued a sell rating due to the weak earnings and limited portfolio growth at the time. Since my last coverage, PSBD's share price has fallen by nearly 19%, and I believe that there is still some downside risk. PSBD is set to release its Q4 earnings at the end of this month, so I wanted to revisit this business development company to reassess its overall value proposition throughout 2026. Unfortunately, I do not believe there to be any meaningful catalyst in the near term, which means that earnings may remain flat and the NAV may continue to decline.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that PSBD's share price has declined by 28.1% and failed to participate in the positive market momentum over the last few quarters. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return still sits at a loss of 18.4% over the same time frame. Due to the continuous decline in share price, PSBD now offers a starting dividend yield of 14.6%. However, I remain cautious about the overall sustainability of the payouts over the next twelve months.

PSBD now trades at its deepest discount to NAV pricing ever, but I do not see this as an opportunity to accumulate. I believe that the portfolio may continue to erode in the near term and the higher interest rate environment may continue to suppress its growth potential. PBSD's NAV continues to decline, and this is a clear indication of some underlying flaws that need to be addressed. Although PSBD's non-accruals remain low, it seems that management has struggled to allocate a meaningful level of capital towards new investment activity.

Financials & Earnings Estimate

According to the latest portfolio breakdown, PSBD has investments at a fair value of $1.25B that are spread across a diverse range of debt securities. Unlike traditional equities, PSBD's success does not rely on the earnings growth of its assets. Instead, PSBD essentially lends capital to a wide range of middle-market borrowers and relies on their ability to keep up with their debt maintenance. A borrower's ability to repay their loans is typically a much easier calculation for management to make, but the success can be influenced by the interest rate environment.

PSBD leans most heavily on senior secured first lien debt, which is ideal because this form of debt sits at the top of the corporate capital structure and has the highest priority for repayment. So in a scenario where a borrower is struggling to keep up with debt payments and defaults, PSBD would be entitled to repayment before other forms of debt. This portfolio's breakdown by investment type is as follows:

  • First Lien Debt: 86%
  • Second Lien Debt: 5%
  • Other: 5%
  • Short-Term Investments: 4%

Expanding on this diversity, PSBD also tries to maintain exposure to different industries to help reduce concentration risks. As we can see below, PSBD is most heavily allocated to software securities at 11% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to healthcare and IT services investments, which accounts for 10% and 9% of assets, respectively.

Despite the diversity, this has not helped PSBD produce strong earnings growth over the last year. For instance, PSBD previously reported its Q3 earnings, and the results were a bit mixed. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.43 per share, which was a decline from the prior year's net investment income of $0.48 per share. Additionally, total investment income landed at $31.6M, which was also a decline from the prior year's total investment income of $37.7M.

It seems like the largest driver of the lower earnings was the decline in interest income received from portfolio companies. Interest income amounted to $30.4M in the most recent quarter, which was a decline from the prior year's interest income of $35.7M. This could be attributed to the lower interest rate environment over the last year, as well as failure to offset repayments with new investment activity.

On a more positive note, PSBD was able to slightly offset the lower earnings by reducing some of its operating expenses. Total operating expense amounted to $18M in the last quarter, which was an improvement over the prior year's total operating expenses of $21.5M. Management was able to reduce their incentive fees to help offset the lower earnings. Additionally, the total interest expenses paid were also reduced down to $12.7M, which is a sign that management has been actively trying to reduce their overall debt burden.

PSBD is set to report their Q4 earnings on February 26th, and I do not have the confidence that there will be substantial improvement. The balance sheet doesn't necessarily indicate management's ability to increase earnings, since cash and equivalents only amounted to $4.2M in the last quarter, while total notes still sit around $302M.

The projected earnings estimate sits around $0.42 per share, which I believe to be reasonable. While I don't think there have been any growth catalysts in the last quarter, I also don't think there are any major red flags within its portfolio that can cause the earnings to substantially decline. Therefore, I anticipate net investment income to land somewhere between the $0.38 to $0.42 per share range for its Q4 earnings.

Valuation & Risks

When I previously covered PSBD, it traded at a discount to NAV of 11.5% at the time. Following the continuous decline in share price, PSBD now trades at a much deeper discount to NAV of 23.47%. For reference, PSBD has only traded at an average discount to NAV of 9.68% over the last three-year period. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that PSBD now trades at its deepest discount to NAV valuation since its inception. Despite this discount, I do not believe this is a good time to accumulate, as I believe there are more downside risks throughout 2026.

Wall Street analysts currently have an average price target of $12.90 per share, which indicates a potential upside of ~13.6% from the current level. However, I disagree with this outlook because I don't see any short-term catalysts that can take the price higher. Management has struggled to allocate a meaningful amount of capital towards new investment activity over the last year. For instance, PSBD was able to allocate $138.6M towards new investments last quarter. However, this was offset by $156M in sales and repayments, resulting in a net decrease of $17.3M for the quarter.

The failure to produce new investments that outpace repayments means that the NAV will continue to decline. A declining NAV is one of the clearest indications of a structural flaw within a BDC's portfolio. As the NAV declines, the underlying share price will also decline and erode an investor's capital over time. For reference, here are the quarter-end NAV amounts over the last year:

  • Q3 2024: $16.61 per share
  • Q4 2024: $16.50 per share
  • Q1 2025: $15.85 per share
  • Q2 2025: $15.68 per share
  • Q3 2025: $15.39 per share

So although PBSD's non-accruals are relatively low, this doesn't necessarily translate to an efficient portfolio strategy. Non-accruals represent the amount of portfolio companies that are struggling to keep up with their required debt maintenance. A portfolio company that is put into non-accrual status is not actively contributing to a BDC's earnings. Based on the last earnings call, management confirmed that non-accruals only amounted to 0.40% of fair value.

While credit quality is a top concern across the sector, non-accruals continue to be low at PSBD. On a fair value basis, it is only 40 basis points and on an at-cost basis, only 101 basis points. Our PIK income as a percentage of total investment income remains well below our largest peers and below the industry at approximately 1.14%. We take pride in knowing our shareholders do not have to wonder about the quality of our disclosed investment income.

- Matthew Bloomfield, President

Despite this, I believe that the higher interest rate environment is the biggest threat to PSBD's success. A higher interest rate means that the cost of debt is high, so there are fewer businesses seeking debt financing. This sort of environment limits the amount of deals available for PSBD to capitalize on, which limits growth potential. Despite the Fed initiating several interest rate cuts throughout 2025, the federal funds rate still remains near the higher end of its ten-year range.

Tighter Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.36 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 14.6%. Alongside the base distribution, management also announced a supplemental distribution of $0.07 per share that was issued to help pay out excess income generated from its positions. As previously mentioned, net investment income amounted to $0.43 per share for the quarter. Although this indicates that earnings support the distributions being paid, I am still cautious about the overall sustainability of payouts going forward.

I would actually argue that management's decision to pay out the excess income above the distribution amount is actually hurting its ability to regain some positive NAV momentum. Instead of paying out the extra capital to investors, this cash flow can be reallocated back into growth positions that would result in positive earnings growth. Since PSBD isn't strategically using this capital to offset the flat earnings, I do not think this is a great position for investors seeking a reliable source of income.

The BDC environment can change rapidly, and even though non-accruals are low right now, this may not always be the case. Although I think it's unlikely, a scenario where interest rates rise can result in further portfolio deterioration. Or in a scenario where interest rate cuts do not happen this year, a higher rate environment may continue to put pressure on existing portfolio companies and limit returns for PSBD.

Lastly, investors need to consider the fact that distributions received from PSBD may not offer tax efficiency. Since distributions are being supported by interest income received from borrowers, the income is classified as ordinary dividends, which have the greatest tax consequences for investors. Therefore, a position in PSBD would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my sell rating on PSBD going into the BDC's Q4 earnings. I do not see any short-term catalysts that can reverse the NAV declines. The BDC is not allocating sufficient capital towards new investment activity that can improve its underlying earnings. Additionally, management has chosen to pay out earnings above the distribution amount as a supplemental distribution, which I don't believe is the best use of capital. So despite PSBD now trading at its deepest discount to NAV valuation ever, I believe the risk is not worth the high yield.

data.title: Huntington Ingalls Industries: Late To The Naval Defense Party, But I Am Upgrading
data.description:
Huntington Ingalls Industries (HII) stock prices have increased almost 150% since my last report. I had a hold rating on HII and that means that while holding the stock definitely would not have been a bad thing, overall this is a missed opportunity as the stock price performance looks more like a buy. In this report, I discuss the company’s Q4 2025 earnings, the outlook for 2026, and I update my price target and rating as aerospace and defense is entering a supercycle, which in my view justifies a buy rating along with improving throughput.

Strong Growth For Huntington Ingalls Industries In Q4 2025

HII sales grew 15.7% in Q4 2025 to $3.5 billion, exceeding analyst estimates by $377.3 million, or 12.2%. Ingalls Shipbuilding revenues rose 20.8% to $889 million, driven by higher volumes on amphibious assault ships and surface combatant programs. Newport New Shipbuilding revenues increased 19.1% to $1.9 billion driven by higher submarine and aircraft carrier volumes. Mission Technologies sales rose 2.5% to $731 million, driven by higher warfare systems and unmanned system sales.

Segment operating earnings surged 89.3%, from $103 million to $195 million, with margins increasing from 3.4% to 5.6%. Ingalls Shipbuilding profits increased almost 50% to $84 million, driven by higher volumes and lower unfavourable cost adjustments. This drove the margins from 6.3% to 7.6%. Newport News Shipbuilding profits rose from $38 million to $84 million, driven by higher volumes and favorable cost adjustments for the Virginia-class submarine compared to unfavourable adjustment on the contracts for the Virginia-class and Columbia-class submarines in the same quarter last year. Mission Technologies profits surged from $19 million to $43 million, driven by higher volume and better performance.

Why Huntington Ingalls Industries Stock Surged

I believe that the stock price of HII surged for several reasons. The first one is the throughput, which increased 14%. Previously the pressure item for HII was simply that while demand was high, the shipbuilding and submarine supply chain was heavily constrained, and that also added costs. The fourth quarter and full-year performance give a strong indication that shipbuilding and submarine programs are largely derisked and the flow is improving. Additionally, on the back of higher defense spending, the prospects for aerospace and defense companies, and especially for the more capital-intensive ship and submarine builders, have substantially improved. This provides a solid base for a rerating of the stock prices. Additionally, with interventions in Venezuela and power projections in Iran, we are seeing that aircraft carriers are not obsolete. This at some point seems to have been the administration’s stance, but we note that President Trump has been a proponent of building a stronger naval fleet. That was the case during his first term and his second term does not deviate from that in my view.

Low Growth Outlook For HII In 2026

For 2026, HII is aiming for $9.7-$9.9 billion in revenues compared to $9.6 billion in 2025. At the midpoint, this marks 2.2% growth. Margins are guided at 5.5%-6.5% compared to margins of 5.9% in 2025. Mission Technologies revenues are guided at $3.0-$3.2 billion in 2026 compared to $3.04 billion in sales for 2025. Margins are expected to be 5%, which is stable year-on-year. At the mid-point, HII is guiding for a 3.3% increase in sales and a 3.6% increase in operating profits with free cash flow of $500-$600 million compared to $800 million in 2025. So, the full-year outlook is not necessarily an impressive one and is unlikely to be a major fundamental driver to the stock price targets.

Huntington Ingalls Industries Stock Is A Buy On An Industry Rerating

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The growth figures for Huntington Ingalls Industries are not necessarily impressive. Revenues are set to grow 5.3% annually with a 10.8% EBITDA growth. However, driven by a pull-forward of free cash flow into 2025, the free cash will not show strong growth. For HII, I definitely missed the huge run up. That run-up is not necessarily driven by better-than-expected results, but by a rerating as for aerospace and defense companies. The obvious risk is that if that re-rating does not hold, upside would evaporate. The upside is currently around 8%, and that doesn’t match my criteria for a buy rating as I typically look for 10% upside. However, we note that annualized upside does meet the 10% criterion. So I am marking the stock with a buy rating and a $450.72 price target, noting 23% upside against earnings expectations for 2028 and even a modest correction on the share price would offer a more compelling entry point.

Margins are expected to increase in the years ahead, driven by higher throughput driving operating leverage. Furthermore, the revenue mix will increasingly lean on post-pandemic contract awards from 2027 onward. That also means that cost overhang, such as inflationary pressures on fixed-price contracts, will slowly but surely fade bringing the company into a stronger margin position. The company’s yield is not necessarily impressive, but I do believe that the company’s substantial improvement in the net debt leverage could result in HII restarting share repurchases, which currently have not been modeled in.

Conclusion: Huntington Ingalls Stock Has Done Surprisingly Well

Looking at the stock price performance, I can only say that I adjusted the rating on the stock too early. I had not expected that the stock would see substantial tailwinds from improved sentiment. However, with global expanding defense budgets, it is undeniable that many aerospace and defense industries are seeing a rerating on their stock prices, and that drives the buy rating for Huntington Ingalls Industries, along with improving throughput, indicating that the industrial base is incrementally improving its ability to meet demand.

data.title: Micron: DDR5 Prices Surge +400% Since September And Could Rise Further In 2026
data.description:
Still Trading at 12x forward P/E

Micron Technology, Inc.'s (MU) stock has rallied +60% since my last rating. The key catalyst behind this relentless rally is the supply constraint on memory chips, which gives the company significant pricing power.

In January, they announced a $100 billion New York "megafab" site with the goal of producing 40% of its DRAM in the U.S. (DRAM represents 79% of total revenue). They also announced a $24 billion Singapore capacity expansion plan. These investments have boosted optimism that MU's growth outlook will be tremendous if it can quickly narrow the supply gap.

Assuming robust AI demand in 2026, we can see this strong growth outlook has already been priced into MU's P/E multiple, which is expected to drop from 36x (based on trailing 12 months EPS) to only 12x (based on forward 12 months EPS consensus).

In my previous analysis, despite the stock jumping 130% from the August 2025 low, I still upgraded MU to Strong Buy before its 1Q FY2026 earnings for three reasons: increasing HBM and DRAM demand, tremendous fundamental improvements (such as margins, earnings, and FCF), and 14x non-GAAP P/E fwd. Although it can be intimidating to be bullish after seeing a parabolic rally in such a short period, MU's forward P/E has become even 14% cheaper since my last rating. This suggests that its near-term earnings growth potential can surpass the recent price appreciation. If the outlook doesn't deviate from current consensus, MU is still a Strong Buy, as there is still room for further multiple expansion.

The Recent Tech Selloff Unlikely Affect MU

As AI continues to evolve, it's inevitable that some industries or companies could face severe disruptions. For example, the 21% YTD pullback in the iShares Expanded Tech-Software Sector ETF (IGV) reflects growing concerns that many software companies are at risk of being replaced by AI. The AI company, Anthropic, released its new AI model, showing that the AI coding agent can write and debug code, which has potential to "destroy" the traditional application software industry. Meanwhile, OpenAI announced its new version of the AI coding agent, Codex-Spark. These announcements spooked the market, triggering a broad-based tech selloff.

However, AI hardware stocks have significantly outperformed this year because better AI performance will require more powerful hardware to support faster computing and other capabilities. Therefore, as long as AI demand remains strong, I believe MU's growth will have room to improve, and the stock could continue to perform well.

Gaining Market Share

MU faces two major competitors in the global memory market: SK hynix and Samsung. Both companies' stocks have also experienced tremendous rallies over the past six months. Together, these three companies dominate the memory industry. According to the recent comments from Morgan Stanley, "DDR5 spot pricing is up another 30% YTD and currently 130% above the January contract, up 86% from December." Given the increasing supply constraint, they expect that mainstream pricing to rise further and could potentially double again this year.

Industry competition has also intensified. According to Counterpoint Research, SK hynix still holds the largest market share as a global top DRAM supplier. The company will provide its HBM products for Nvidia (NVDA)'s early Rubin volume. However, as shown on the chart above, MU is catching up. The company has gained significant DRAM market share from 21% to 26% within a 12-month period.

In addition, MU's HBM market share has doubled from 11% to 22% in a year. During the recent Wolfe Research Auto, Auto Tech and Semiconductor Conference 2026 in February, management noted that the company's strong financial outlook has been driven by a "favorable price environment". MU's HBM4 is receiving strong demand from NVDA. I believe the company has potential to secure more orders if they can ramp up HBM4 production faster.

Strong Pricing Power, Gross Margin Jump

The DRAM and HBM pricing momentums have been incredibly strong, but are they sustainable? The short answer is likely not in the long term. We would eventually see some margin normalization at some point. When supply constraints ease, prices will decrease as volumes increase. However, in the near term, more price hikes will boost the gross margin. The company previously guided 51.5% non-GAAP gross margin for 1Q FY2026. The actual number came in at 58.8%. Moreover, its 2Q FY2026 gross margin is expected to jump even higher to a new all-time high of 68%.

Let's look at earnings. MU beat its previous 1Q FY2026 EPS guidance by 27%. After three consecutive quarters of triple digit growth in non-GAAP EPS, the 1Q result suggested a sequential growth acceleration to 167% YoY. Management even guided for nearly 440% YoY growth in 2Q FY2026. This explains why the stock has experienced such a parabolic rally over the past few months.

However, there is a catch: the company's guidance and market consensus are estimated, which means that these numbers can turn out to be totally wrong if we see an unexpectedly slowdown in AI demand and exogenous shocks to the semiconductor industry, such as policy and macro risks.

How to Interpret This Cheap Valuation

MU's current valuation is a little tricky. Since September last year, the stock has rallied nearly 250%. During the same period, SK hynix and Samsung's stock jumped 220% and 75%, respectively. On a TTM basis, SK hynix's P/E is 14x and Samsung's is 27x, according to Seeking Alpha. Given the current revenue growth and margin outlook, their forward P/E ratios will be significantly lower.

As mentioned earlier, the forward P/E has dropped to 12x, even lower than 14x two months ago, despite a 60% rally. This basically tells that the stock has fully priced in the continued price squeeze on global memory chips due to supply constraints.

Based on Memory Price Trends, overall DDR5 pricing has jumped by more than 400% since September 2025. The question is whether these prices will reverse or stay elevated. MU's forward valuation multiples look very cheap because of this trend. If AI demand slows sharply, prices could drop. This would make the current earnings consensus too high. As those estimates are revised downward, MU's valuation could suddenly become much more expensive.

"Fat Tail" Risk and "Value Trap"

Based on what we've just discussed about MU's product price tailwind, let's dive a little deeper into the company's "fat tail" risk. I believe that risk could be more devastating given the magnitude of the recent rally. This is not a bubble. Like I said, if these product prices reverse significantly, MU's growth and margin will likely normalize. Trading at 12x forward P/E could explain why investors haven't pushed the multiple higher yet, because they believe this cheap valuation is unsustainable and could be a "value trap". In this context, I define a value trap not as deterioration in MU's business but as a normalization from an extreme growth momentum.

Conclusion

In summary, MU's recent rally since September has been largely driven by favorable pricing and supply constraints on DRAM and HBM. The market believes that AI demand remains robust in 2026. DDR5 prices have gone up by more than 400% within six months, and the consensus indicates prices could double again in 2026. That's why MU can maintain a triple-digit EPS growth and even will accelerate strongly in 2Q FY2026. If this trend can continue, I believe MU's valuation still looks cheap for FY2026. Given this, I reiterate Strong Buy on the stock.

However, the key downside risk is that these elevated prices may not be sustainable if AI demand unexpectedly weakens, but for now, it's still a tail risk.

data.title: The Optimist Fund Q4 2025 Quarterly Letter
data.description:
To the Partners of Optimist Fund,

When we launched Optimist in 2022, our objective was explicit. Deliver exceptional long term investment performance. We articulate this as compounding capital at mid teens or better over decades. That goal remains unchanged and it is what we wake up every morning working toward.

Optimist has been operating for just under four years. While we have not yet completed our first five-year vintage, an important point given that we invest with a five-year lens, we are tracking in a direction I am pleased with.

% Returns Optimist Fund* Benchmark**
2025 32.2% 8.9%
Q4 2025 -8.5% -2.0%
Q3 2025 9.1% 7.9%
Q2 2025 39.2% 11.3%
Q1 2025 -4.9% -7.5%
2024 66.5% 34.1%
2023 82.9% 27.4%
2022*** -51.4% -11.8%
Compound Returns as of December 31st, 2025
1 Year 32.2% 8.9%
2 Year 48.4% 20.8%
3 Year 59.1% 23.0%
Since Inception 19.1% 13.8%
***Fund start date was March 1, 2022.

In Q4, Optimist declined 8.5 percent. While short-term drawdowns are never enjoyable, we remain highly encouraged by the fundamental performance of the businesses we own and have strong conviction in our prospective returns over the next five years.

Before walking through Q4 attribution and our positioning for 2026, I want to address an important higher-level question that I know sits in the minds of some investors.

1. Why have we been able to deliver market leading performance over the past four years?

2. Why do we believe that performance is repeatable over the next five and beyond?

Why We've Outperformed

We're Focused

At the core of our results is a very specific focus. Identifying businesses where deep, targeted research can uncover a meaningful gap between market expectations and long-term reality. We refer to this as developing unique insights.

If the market believes a company will generate five billion dollars of profit in five years and values it accordingly, but our research suggests it is far more likely to earn ten billion dollars over that same period, the outcome, if we are right, tends to be strong investment returns.

The obvious follow up question is how we consistently identify what the market is missing.

We do this by focusing on specific types of companies which can be overlooked or misunderstood for specific reasons we have come to understand.

We refer to these opportunities as Compounders in Disguise and Inflecting Compounders, which we discussed in depth at our Investor Day and in our Q2 2025 Investor Letter. By deliberately focusing on these specific types of businesses, where we have developed deep domain expertise, we increase the likelihood of uncovering differentiated insight and, in turn, opportunities to materially outperform over the long term. This discipline, knowing where we can have an edge and ignoring the rest, is a core reason we believe our results have been strong and, more importantly, repeatable.

We Play the Long Game

From day one, we have been explicit that Optimist is run to maximize five-year investment outcomes, not quarterly results. As a result, stock price volatility is not something we fear. In fact, it is often an amplifier of long-term performance.

The bear markets of 2022 and 2025 allowed us to rebalance aggressively into businesses whose long-term fundamentals remained intact, but whose share prices were indiscriminately punished.

Being comfortable with short term volatility is a requirement to successfully execute our investment approach and to continue delivering the type of returns we have in the past.

Over the last four years, executing against this approach led us to add meaningfully to positions such as Carvana (CVNA), Wayfair (W), ThredUp (TDUP), DoorDash (DASH), and Uber (UBER) during periods of extreme share price weakness. Those decisions have been very profitable. It has also led us to add to positions such as HelloFresh (HLFFF) and Peloton (PTON), which were not good investments.

This brings me to an important final point as to why we have been able to generate strong long-term results.

We Acknowledge and Accept That We Will Not Get Everything Right

And importantly, we do not need to. The capital generated from our collection of correct investment decisions more than offsets inevitable losses from our mistakes, leaving us with attractive long-term investment returns.

Why We Believe Outperformance Is Repeatable

Many investors deliver strong early results by being a big fish in a small pond, only to lose their edge as assets grow and their opportunity set shrinks. Though our assets under management have increased over ten-fold over the last three years, our strategy can scale to at least 20x our current AUM.

The average market capitalization of our holdings has always exceeded five billion dollars and today sits above thirty billion dollars USD. Our edge does not depend on exploiting microcap or small cap inefficiencies. It comes from differentiated fundamental insight in global category leading businesses whose market capitalizations are most often measured in the many billions.

Furthermore, our results have not been driven by riding thematic cycles, whether commodities, AI, or broader technology waves. Our outperformance has come from idiosyncratic, company specific insights that are largely independent of macro or sector rotations.

Optimist launched in 2022, one of the most challenging periods for equity investors, particularly for those focused on higher-growth companies. Under significant pressure, many managers capitulated and exited businesses such as Carvana, DoorDash, Uber, Netflix (NFLX), and Meta (META) after share prices declined more than fifty percent at their lows. Instead, they rotated into companies perceived as safer simply because their share prices had fallen less. That decision proved costly, as many of those same businesses have meaningfully outperformed since the end of 2022.

As a result, those managers underperformed on the way down and again on the way up.

Our results were not the product of fortunate timing. They were the result of remaining disciplined to our investment philosophy when doing so was most difficult.

Stepping back, it should be clear that our edge is not accidental or dependent on a fleeting market condition. We know what we are good at. We are good at identifying misunderstood compounders, developing differentiated insight through deep research, and acting decisively when volatility creates opportunity. That skill set is not transitory. It is rooted in process, temperament, and focus.

Sustained long-term outperformance will never simply happen on its own. It requires continued intellectual honesty, relentless work, and a willingness to be uncomfortable and unpopular when the data supports it. We are well equipped for that challenge. More importantly, we are energized by it.

Looking Ahead

We believe the next five years will be better than the last four.

Not because of a top-down positive macro economic view but because current valuations, in our view, materially underappreciate the earnings and cash flow our core holdings will generate five years from now.

That gap is measurable. Consensus expectations remain anchored to prior periods, while the underlying businesses have materially improved. Cost structures are leaner, balance sheets are stronger, unit economics are clearer and sales and earnings growth are accelerating.

We are seeing this in real-time.

Optimist Fund's best days are ahead.

Q4 Review

Top Q4 Contributors Contribution Top Q4 Detractors Contribution
Wayfair 1.5% ThredUp -7.1%
Carvana 1.5% Monday.com (MNDY) -1.4%
Latham -1.0%
Top Contributor Total 3.0% Top Detractor Total -9.5%

Top Contributors

Wayfair – Last quarter, we highlighted that Wayfair's Q2 results were the strongest we had seen in several years and supported our thesis that the business was positioned for a meaningful rebound as the home furnishings market recovered. Q3 built on that momentum. Revenue grew 9% year over year, accelerating from 6% in Q2, while adjusted EBITDA increased more than 70% year over year. These results provide further evidence that Wayfair's future profit growth remains substantial and is materially underappreciated by the market. Wayfair continues to be a top five holding.

Carvana – Carvana's strong financial trajectory continues reporting record third-quarter results, with 155,941 retail cars sold (+~44% YoY) and total revenue of ~$5.65 billion (+~55% YoY), both all-time quarterly highs. The company generated adjusted EBITDA of ~$637 million +~50%, with an adjusted EBITDA margin of ~11.3%, well above typical industry profitability levels. Carvana remains a top 5 holding.

Top Detractors

ThredUp – ThredUp delivered an excellent third quarter, with revenue up approximately 34% year over year, one of the fastest growth rates in its recent history. Adjusted EBITDA improved to $3.8 million, or a 4.6% margin, from roughly breakeven last year, while active buyers increased about 26%, driven by strong new buyer acquisition. Management also raised full year revenue guidance, signaling confidence in the outlook.

Notably, management highlighted that new buyer growth accelerated to 81% year over year in October, up from 54% in Q3, following a rebrand and the launch of a new product recommendation feed in late September.

While fundamentals continue to strengthen, the stock has declined over the past five months. These dislocations between price and fundamentals are common in the types of businesses we invest in and have led us to add to the position. ThredUp remains our largest holding.

Monday.com – In November, Monday.com reported a mixed quarter, with revenue and free cash flow growing approximately 26% and 10% year over year, respectively. Enterprise performance continued to accelerate, while growth in small and mid sized customers further decelerated.

We held the position through year end and into 2026, believing management could execute against the objectives they had outlined, supported by a strong historical track record. However, following the company's fourth quarter earnings release earlier this week, we have fully exited the position. The reported results were not aligned with our forecasts, and more importantly, management was unable to clearly articulate what is occurring within the business.

When we lose confidence in the accuracy or clarity of management's communication, we exit. That is what we have done here.

Latham Group (SWIM) – Latham delivered a solid third quarter, with net sales of approximately $162 million, up ~7.6% year-over-year, outperforming a generally flat U.S. in-ground pool market and reflecting continued traction across key product lines, including pool covers, liners, and fiberglass pools. Adjusted EBITDA itself grew roughly 28.5% to about $38.3 million. Latham also narrowed and modestly raised its full-year guidance, signaling confidence in its outlook despite broader industry headwinds, with net sales now expected in the ~$540–$550 million range and adjusted EBITDA of ~$92–$98 million. Overall, the quarter demonstrated a business that continues to grow revenue, expand profitability, and execute on strategic priorities even in challenging end-market conditions. We are pleased with results.

On the negative side, Latham's CEO, Scott Rajeski, announced his retirement. The incoming CEO appears credible, coming from James Hardie, a much larger organization, but we have not yet had the opportunity to engage with him and plan to do so following year-end results. Given the unexpected leadership change, we reduced the position from 6% to 3% of the fund. This was not driven by a change in our view of the business, but by our emphasis on management quality and familiarity. Until we build conviction in the new leadership, our overall conviction is lower.

Aggregate Portfolio Composition

Here are the top 10 holdings in our fund, comprising approximately 85% of the portfolio as of December 31 st, 2025.

ThredUp Uber
Carvana Monday.com
Wayfair Latham Group
First Advantage (FA) DiscoverIE (DCVNF)
DoorDash Root (ROOT)

Other Notable Changes

During the quarter, we exited our position in Fiverr (FVRR), which had previously been among our top ten holdings. We also initiated new positions in Root, a disruptive insurance company, and Affirm (AFRM), the leading buy now pay later provider in the United States. In addition, we increased our investment in DiscoverIE, elevating it to a top ten holding.

Our investment in Fiverr was based on the belief that the company could reaccelerate organic revenue growth back into the mid-teens, which we believed would drive a meaningful re-rating of the shares. Under that scenario, our five-year valuation target was well over $100 per share, compared to a share price of roughly $20 as of December 31st.

In Q4, we made the decision that we would rather own Fiverr at a higher price with greater conviction that their growth will reaccelerate than own it today at a lower price with less conviction. Put differently, we would be comfortable purchasing the shares at $30 if we had stronger evidence that revenue growth is sustainably accelerating and that our longer-term ~$100+ valuation target is increasingly likely to be realized.

This decision reflects our discipline around conviction and opportunity cost. While we do not own Fiverr today, it remains on our watchlist, and we would not hesitate to re-establish a position should the potential accelerating operating trends, begin to materialize.

DiscoverIE: A High-Quality Industrial Compounder at an Inflection Point

DiscoverIE Group plc is a leading designer and manufacturer of highly differentiated electronic components for mission critical industrial applications. The company specializes in bespoke, application specific products, including magnetics, sensors, power electronics, and connectivity solutions, that are designed into customer systems and typically remain in place for the life of the product. Examples include custom temperature sensors used in specialized medical devices and X ray sensors embedded in airport security scanners. These niche, high value components are small in cost but critical to system performance and reliability.

The strategy is simple and disciplined: operate in structurally growing end markets, focus on low-volume, high-complexity components that are difficult to switch, and compound growth through organic design wins and bolt-on acquisitions. This model has enabled 20%+ EPS and free cash flow per share growth over the past 10 years.

While challenging end markets led to negative organic growth in both fiscal 2024/fiscal 2025 and compressed the valuation from over 25x earnings to ~15x today, we believe the business is entering the early stages of a cyclical recovery. In our view, this supports the potential for ~20% EPS growth over the next five years alongside meaningful multiple expansion.

With aligned management, disciplined capital allocation, and a focused growth strategy, DiscoverIE fits squarely within the type of under-appreciated industrial technology compounder we seek to own.

Closing Remarks

I hope after reading this letter, you feel you have a better understanding of what we are doing at Optimist and why we remain so excited about the opportunity ahead.

Thank you to everyone who joined us at our 2025 Year in Review Webinar a few weeks ago. If you missed it, click here to watch the replay.

We deeply appreciate your continued trust and partnership. As always, please feel free to reach out with any questions.

Speak soon,

Jordan McNamee

Founder & Chief Investment Officer

data.title: Rates Spark: Quiz Time
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas and Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist

Here's how we view the curve now and in the period ahead

Essentially, the 10yr yield has been up and down in the past few years, briefly above 4.5% at times, and below 4% more often, but by and large has not shown a tendency to radically change its averaging at or about 4.2%.

And that, by the way, is at or about the neutral tendency that we identify for the 10yr Treasury yield. In other words, if the funds rate was close to neutral and inflation close to neutral, we should expect the 10yr yield to be around neutral too.

Currently, we have a funds rate at 3.64%, just a tad tight versus neutrality. But the market is discounting a return to neutrality over the course of 2026, which helps to pitch the 2yr yield at 3.3%. The totality of circumstances on the front end generally smacks of neutrality.

And inflation? We saw that printing at or about 2.5% last week. And inflation expectations tend to be in that territory too (or slightly below for longer tenors). That's all consistent with this notion of neutrality when it comes to pitching where the 10yr yield should be.

What about bond supply, and the fiscal deficit? Two things. First, the market does not seem to care. Second, the effect on longer tenors is being curtailed through reliance on heavier short-tenor issuance. What about Kevin Warsh's ambition to reduce the balance sheet?

If true, it must mean the Fed holds less and the market then needs to hold more. That adds to the weight-of-supply story. There is an offset coming, however, from the 1 April loosening in the leverage ratio requirements for the large US banks, which should allow them to hold more Treasuries (should they choose to do so). Overall, the theme here is mixed to bearish, but far from determinative.

And what about the slowdown risk for the US economy? This is such an interesting debate. There is a significant expectation for relatively poor macro circumstances ahead. But not poor enough, it seems, to push the funds rate expectation structurally and materially below 3%.

But if that is true, why the predominant expectation for macro weakness? The market discount is telling us that things are quite nuanced. We're left with the impression that the 10yr can very easily hot-tail back to where it came from in the 4.2% area, unless we hear or know more.

Wednesday’s events and market view

After a slow start, the US data calendar is getting busier with the release of the durable goods orders, housing start numbers and industrial production data – the consensus is looking for steady numbers.

The FOMC minutes of the January meeting could be more interesting since the Fed displayed a more hawkish tinge. The minutes could reveal to what extent this was more aimed at stemming the dollar weakness at that point in time. The Fed’s Bowman is slated to speak at a supervision and regulation-themed event.

There are no releases of note from the eurozone with only France releasing final CPI data for January. Of more interest will be the speeches of the ECB's Villeroy, Cipollone and Schnabel – the latter speaking on sovereign debt levels.

In primary markets, Germany will sell €5.5bn in 10y Bunds while the US Treasury will sell US$16bn in new 20y bonds.

data.title: Nomura International Core Equity Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

International equities, as represented by the MSCI All Country World Index ('ACWI') ex USA Index, continued to display solid performance through 4Q2025, with the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index (NET) extending gains by 5.1% as global investors increasingly allocate outside the US amid attractive valuations and supportive macro dynamics. Broad international stocks – including developed and emerging markets – have meaningfully outpaced US equities year to date, underpinned by a weaker US dollar and improving earnings expectations across key regions.

At the country level, parts of Europe (particularly the UK and Switzerland) were notable contributors, buoyed by export resilience. The technology-heavy North Asian markets also fared well except for China, which posted weaker economic data. South Korean equities continued to be lifted by domestic policy and governance reforms momentum and improving earnings visibility from tech demand, while the rest of emerging markets added breadth. Sectoral divergence was clear this quarter: financials and traditional cyclicals led, contrasting with underperformance by growth-oriented sectors including consumer discretionary and communication services. This amplified the value-led leadership theme across international markets.

Within the Fund

In 4Q25, Nomura International Core Equity Fund posted a positive total return but underperformed the Fund's benchmark, the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index (net).

The quarter was marked by strong outperformance of value over growth stocks, which was a headwind for the Fund's core approach. As regards stock selection, it proved challenging across industrials, consumer discretionary, and healthcare, with prior longtime outperformers selling as their growth leadership moderated. In industrials, the Fund's defense exposures and consumer credit rating agency retraced in favor of more cyclical names, including machinery, construction, and building products companies most notably in Japan. Many of these names have traded as artificial intelligence ('AI') derivatives due to business exposure to the AI supply chain, which raised their valuations. Within consumer discretionary, the Fund's exposure to internet platform companies was negative while its overweight to Swiss multinational healthcare companies detracted as these names saw earnings resiliency despite tariff concerns and high valuations.

The Fund has experienced two very challenging quarters in a row, which had led to a significant valuation reset for many of our top holdings. We believe this will set them up for future success as growth continues. While our convictions have not been reduced for most of these holdings, the Fund eliminated exposure to stocks in which fundamentals have changed. These included Nintendo Co. Ltd. (NTDOY), Haeleon PLC, Makita Corp. (MKTAY), Siemens AG (SIEGY), and Mondi PLC (MONDY) amid a combination of cost, macro pressures, or simply what we perceived to be rich valuations for moderate growth. In turn, we initiated several new positions, including Barclays PLC (BCS), Olympus Corp. (OCPNY), Embraer SA (ERJ), and argenx SE (ARGX).

Among individual stocks, notable outperformers were SK Hynix (Korea), Barclays PLC ('UK'), and Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria SA (BBVA) ('BBVA') (Spain):

  • SK Hynix (SKHHY), a market leader in memory semiconductors, notably High Bandwidth Memory ('HBM') essential for AI infrastructure, was buoyed by strong 3Q earnings driven by strong demand and rising prices. The market perception of memory semiconductors is moving beyond the boom-bust commodity trade to their key role in core AI infrastructure.
  • Barclays, one of the UK's largest banks, benefited from continued positive sentiment toward European financials and strong 3Q financial results that prompted upgraded performance targets and an accelerated share buyback program.
  • Shares of BBVA, a leading banking franchise across Spain and Latin America, rallied further, driven primarily by a renewed focus on significant shareholder returns (dividends and substantial share buybacks) following the failure of its bid for Banco Sabadell, along with strong operational performance in its key markets of Mexico and Spain.

Notable individual detractors were Sea Ltd. (SE) (Singapore), MercadoLibre (MELI) (Argentina), and Grab Holdings Ltd. (GRAB) All three have distinct yet related reasons tied to competitive pressures, profitability concerns, and valuations dynamics.

  • Shares of MercadoLibre ('MELI'), Latin America's leading ecommerce and digital financial services company, saw further profit-taking during the period after the strong run-up in recent years. Intensifying competition from Amazon's expanding logistics footprint and Sea's Shopee platform in core markets including Brazil has forced MELI into defensive pricing and higher spending. We remain confident in the growth and MELI's dominant regional moat.
  • Sea, a leading consumer internet company at the intersection of rising digital consumption and financial inclusion in Southeast Asia and select global markets, also saw its shares pull back on rising costs and margin pressure despite strong revenue growth across ecommerce (Shopee) and digital financial services. We maintain a positive view based on long-term structural growth drivers, which remain intact, in our view.
  • Grab Holdings, a leading Southeast Asia super-app integrating ride-hailing, food delivery, and other services including financial services, saw its shares decline during the period due to profit-taking and mixed earnings reactions despite strong underlying growth. The company remains a key holding on the back of strength in mobility, continued profitability improvements, and fintech monetization that we believe will continue to support fundamentals growth.

Outlook

The outlook for international markets remains compelling, in our view.

Starting with Europe, the economic outlook continues to be supported by moderate inflation, consumer resilience, and fiscal and infrastructure spending, with capital expenditure increases seen around the energy transition, defense, and technology sectors, which could bolster competitiveness in the long run. Geopolitical tensions have moderated but risks remain around diverging fiscal paths across the regions. We continue to see momentum for defense spending in Europe despite a dip in defense stocks in recent months amid Ukraine security talks. As we previously wrote, NATO leaders agreed to increase defense spending to 5% of gross domestic product ('GDP') (or $1.5 trillion in additional annual spending across NATO). Another area of exposure has been industrials amid tailwinds from Germany's €500 billion infrastructure program, which has the potential to buoy the domestic economy. Overall, earnings consensus growth is strong for 2026 as investors have turned more constructive, driven by attractive valuations and gradually improving macro conditions in Europe.

In Asia, with the exception of Japan, central banks have embraced a dovish stance, made possible by the US Federal Reserve embarking on its own easing cycle. This should support economic activity across the region. Geopolitical concerns have also abated, removing an overhang on markets, although flare-ups are possible; India remains a sticky point while a truce with China has been extended. While Chinese technology stocks have momentum, the economy has much less. We will continue to look for ideas but maintain our valuation discipline. The outlook for Japan is positive based on governance reforms, fiscal support, and the underlying AI-related tailwinds, but we are cautious about various export-oriented companies as the Bank of Japan normalizes rates, potentially reversing the trajectory of a weak yen.

Despite a challenging few months for the portfolio, it is important to remain grounded. We maintain our valuation discipline and continue to devote most of our time assessing companies' fundamentals. Our focus is on companies we believe have long-term compounding characteristics, unrecognized growth potential, and high-quality cyclicals. This should lead to a portfolio with a diverse set of exposures and risk characteristics, which primarily generates returns through stock selection as opposed to country or thematic exposure.

We believe international markets represent a differentiated opportunity set of leading companies in sectors less prominent than in the US. Despite outperformance in 2025, international markets have been ignored for many years and continue to present attractive opportunities related to their US counterparts, in our view. We believe there is a chance for momentum unwind in the US, with a growing domestic investors base looking beyond the country for the first time in a long while. We believe there is a real possibility for international markets to catch up to their US counterparts in the next few years.
